topics: 🕳️ Pit Mishnah: 10 tefachim is the death threshold
📜 Two pesukim — כי יפתח and כי יכרה — for two kinds of bor
🤲 Acquired pit: bought, inherited, or gifted — still your bor
🤝 Pit of two partners — when does each one become liable?
🪨 'A truck full of stones' — the standard of proper covering

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Defining the Pit
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: Mishnah 5:6 and 5:7 unfold the rules of the bor — first defining when a hole counts as a death-hazard (10 tefachim), then asking who pays in a partnership pit. The Yerushalmi anchors both pesukim of Shemot 21 (יפתח and יכרה) into a system, then asks the natural follow-up: if you didn't dig but acquired the pit, are you still on the hook? Plus the practical question — how thick a cover counts as 'proper'?
overview.hebrewSource: מַה הַבּוֹר שֶׁהוּא כְדֵי לְהָמִית עַד עֲשָׂרָה טְפָחִים. אַף כָּל דָּבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ כְדֵי לְהָמִית עַד עֲשָׂרָה טְפָחִים

slides.title: 🪜 The 10-Tefach Death Threshold
slides.subtitle: How the Mishnah extends 'pit' to all hazardous excavations.

boxes.title: 📏 Below 10 Tefachim
items: Animal falls in and dies → פָּטוּר

callout.label: Why 10:
callout.text: The Mishnah uses bor as the paradigm because it's the case the Torah names. The number 10 is then projected outward: anything that יֵשׁ בּוֹ כְדֵי לְהָמִית at that depth carries the same liability.

slides.title: 💬 Two Pesukim — Two Kinds of Pit
slides.subtitle: How the Yerushalmi splits death-liability and damage-liability.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: כְּתִיב כִּי יִפְתַּח אִישׁ בּוֹר וְכִי יִכְרֶה אִישׁ בּוֹר — two pesukim, one for the pit-of-damages and one for the pit-of-death.

lines.speaker: R' Yitz
lines.text: Both are derived from a single source. But they diverge in halakhah: פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים in the death-pit and חַיָיב עַל הַכֵּלִים in the damage-pit.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: And by the second pasuk's word אוֹ כִי יִכְרֶה — we add anyone who acquired the pit (bought, inherited, given as a gift), not only the one who dug it.

callout.label: Key move:
callout.text: Liability for a bor doesn't depend on the act of digging. The Torah's two verbs (פתיחה and כריה) anchor two regimes; the word אוֹ reaches even the passive owner of a pit.

slides.title: 🤝 The Partnership Pit
slides.subtitle: When two people share a pit, who pays?

nodes.content: How can a pit have two partners?
R' Yannai: שֶׁנָּתְנוּ שְׁנֵיהֶן אֶבֶן הָאַחֲרוֹנָה — they together placed the final stone that opened it to 10 tefachim

columns.label: Neither covered
columns.content: First passes, doesn't cover. Second passes, doesn't cover. → Second liable

columns.label: First covered, second found uncovered, didn't recover

nodes.content: Liability transfers when the pit is left open by the most recent person who could have acted

callout.label: Cedar logic:
callout.text: R' Yochanan: we give the second partner enough time to לִכְרוֹת אֲרָזִים מִן הַלְּבָנוֹן — to cut cedars from Lebanon. The window is reasonable, but proximity to Lebanon makes it stricter or more lenient.

slides.title: 📊 Liability Matrix for the Pit
slides.subtitle: The Mishnah's edge cases.
rows: Animal falls forward from sound of digging
Animal falls backward from sound of digging
Cheresh / shoteh / katan ox falls in
Son, daughter, slave, slave-girl falls in

callout.label: Asymmetry:
callout.text: The pit is liable for animals but never for vessels — and never for capable humans. The Yerushalmi reads this from the verse's words: שׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר, not 'a person.'

slides.title: 🪨 What Counts as 'Properly Covered'?
slides.subtitle: The Yerushalmi gives a real-world test.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Mishnah: כִּיסָּהוּ כָּרָאוּי — covered properly. But what does that mean?
2️⃣ Yerushalmi: יָיבֹא כַּיי דְּתַנִּינָן תַּמָּן — same as we said elsewhere.
3️⃣ Standard: כְּדֵי שֶׁתְּהֵא עֲגָלָה מֲהַלֶּכֶת טְעוּנָה אֲבָנִים
4️⃣ Meaning: a wagon loaded with stones can drive across without breaking through.

callout.label: Practical takeaway:
callout.text: The cover isn't just visual — it has to bear weight equivalent to the heaviest realistic load on a public road. That's the bar between liability and exemption when an animal still falls in.

summary.principle: A pit becomes a halakhic hazard at 10 tefachim, and the Torah's two verbs (יפתח and יכרה) frame two liability regimes. Acquiring is like digging; failing to recover an uncovered pit transfers liability to the last responsible person.

flowSteps.content:
🎓 Key Principle:
Pit liability tracks risk, not labor. Whoever owns the open hazard at the moment of damage owns the loss.

quiz.q: At what depth does the Mishnah say a pit becomes a death-hazard?
quiz.e: מַה הַבּוֹר שֶׁהוּא כְדֵי לְהָמִית עַד עֲשָׂרָה טְפָחִים — 10 tefachim is the threshold the Mishnah extends to all excavations.

quiz.q: Why does the Torah have two pesukim — כי יפתח and כי יכרה?
o: One for the pit of damages, one for the pit of death
One for owners, one for diggers
One for animals, one for humans
quiz.e: The Yerushalmi splits them: אֶחָד בּוֹר לִנְזָקִין וְאֶחָד בּוֹר לְמִיתָה.

quiz.q: From where do we learn that someone who bought, inherited, or received a pit is also liable?
o: From the word אוֹ in 'or if a man dig a pit'
quiz.e: שֶׁנֶּאֱמַר אוֹ כִי יִכְרֶה אִישׁ בּוֹר — the conjunctive 'or' extends liability to non-diggers.

quiz.q: How does R' Yannai explain a 'pit of two partners'?
o: They both put in the last stone, together opening it to 10 tefachim
quiz.e: תִּיפְתָּר שֶׁנָּתְנוּ שְׁנֵיהֶן אֶבֶן הָאַחֲרוֹנָה — joint act on the threshold stone makes both partners.

quiz.q: What is the Yerushalmi's standard for a 'properly covered' pit?
o: Strong enough that a wagon loaded with stones can drive over it
Visible from a distance of 10 amos
quiz.e: כְּדֵי שֶׁתְּהֵא עֲגָלָה מֲהַלֶּכֶת טְעוּנָה אֲבָנִים — the cover must bear a fully loaded wagon.
